## [00:00 - 03:00]

Hey, this is Deepesh and I'm going to talk you through the creative fishing strategy, which is a key part of the graduation framework So creative fishing strategy is all about your ad. So when I talk about creative, it's the visual. It's the copy It's the content. It's the offer and everything contained within the ad itself But you know, I don't want to see it as an ad. It's a creative piece And so if you kind of reframe it as a creative it then gets you thinking about the content and the visual as well So it's focused on things like the even in the call to action The format as well image carousel video, etc Now with the creative fishing strategy again, I use page post engagement to test the ads now If you're running a lead gen campaign through Facebook You can also run the creative fishing strategy through lead gen campaign as well using the same concepts I'll take you through and if you're running mobile app installs My preference is to still run it through page post engagement But again, you can run it through a mobile app install campaign as well The key reason why I use the page post engagement campaign is you'll see in the videos further through the course is that you get access To a wider range of people within your audience when you start to limit it to lead gen and mobile app install Facebook is now starting to reduce the circles of people that you can reach within the audience as well And so page post engagement is my preference if you're running videos again You can do it through page post engagement or you can use the video views objective now The video views objective the main difference with PPE is that you get different controls and features for the video campaigns But essentially at its core the page post engagement objective and the video views are not massively different So, you know, you're looking for engagement video views will bias towards people that gonna View a video and so page post engagement kind of keeps that broader. And that's why I prefer to use that as well the The way I kind of run the campaigns is when I'm doing the creative fact fishing strategy I want to pick my best audience and I want to test creators within that now The best audience would be one that I've already Tested through audience spotlighting if you're starting out fresh and you've got no creative You've got no audience for example Then the way I would approach it is to launch multiple audiences Launch multiple creatives and test all of those variations together so that I can at least Surface an audience that I know as a winner and a creative as well And then once I've got my winning audience and I've proven that there is Potential to do to scale and I'm doing well in my main accounts I will bring that back into my page post engagement campaign and then I will apply new creatives against that as well now Let's say for example You have two audiences that are doing really well One is aimed at women of you know, 20 to 40 in age One is aimed at 40 to 16

## [03:00 - 06:03]

You know already based on your previous testing that the creatives within them are very different Then when it comes to creative fishing and finding new creatives to apply I would be testing specific creatives for those audiences in my page post engagement campaign I'm running the ads at five dollars per ad set per day You can do more the reason I do this is because I want to be able to test many many creatives In the background whilst I'm doing my scaling and optimization Etc. Now when you're starting off and you're you're you're creating your spend on Facebook majority of your budget will be for testing But then as you start to increase your spend and Scale up your performance the percentage that you're putting into testing will reduce But the overall spend should should generally even itself out So the reason for going for five dollars per ad set per day is that I want to test multiple multiple Ads and offers at the same time. So, you know, let's say you've got a fifty dollar a day test budget You could do one fifty dollar ad set You could do what five ten dollar ad sets. I prefer to do the Ten five dollar ad set so it gives me more More scope to do testing within that as well When I'm testing per ad set, I will generally push three ads It just gives Facebook some variance to play around with if you absolutely want to split the Creatives out and gets the kind of best results Then you should you should be using the split test feature within Facebook That's the only way within Facebook to take two creatives Split an audience absolutely in half and make sure one One side of that audience only sees one creative the other side of the audience only sees one the kind of lazy way of doing it Is to take the same audience and just create a duplicate of the ad set But it does mean that some audiences may see the other ad as well My preference is just to keep it simple and just keep the multiple creatives in one ad set If I see a bad performer that I'm cutting it if I see a good performer then I'll decide whether to graduate it out of that as well and you later on in the training if you haven't seen it You'll come across the ad sniper strategy, which you can also apply here as well Now when you do have winners, especially if you're doing a website conversion campaign often I will keep my creatives in my Page post engagement campaign so that I'm building up the social proof in the background And then I will duplicate the ad into my into my website conversion campaign using page post ID Then you can kind of just link the ads together and keep the social proof going as well One one thing about the kind of testing philosophy that I work to is a star method of testing the setup tests analyze and repeat There is a training module on that do have a look. It's not essential It's more of a mindset than anything else, but there is all Trello board which supports it as well, but it's just about being in the mindset of

not and then you can sprinkle your own thoughts on top of that as well and the final thing about Testing with your ads is that the first 500 impressions impressions that you get matter Now when Facebook calculates relevance score, for example, it takes the first 500 impressions that it gets To figure out whether people have responded well or not So Facebook are confident in 500 impressions being enough to determine your relevance score right from the start Then there must be something in that for you as well What I would say is Facebook doesn't always get it right so if you have an There's no harm in duplicating that ad and trying again Maybe even duplicating two three times doing what what we call a kind of a a a test So in my conversion rate optimization days Sometimes we times the Conversion rate of one landing page would be better better than the other Even though they are exactly the same landing page and this happens because if you think about sending traffic to the landing pages No one person is the same So you might be in buying mode you might just be in browsing mode There's so many different factors at play in and the same thing can happen with your ad as ## [12:13 - 15:13]

I'm looking for on this setup as well So in this example, I've taken a look alike of 1% of all my customers Which for this e-commerce store was the best audience The product was jackets and the age range 25 45 females So that that was my best audience and what I wanted to do is to find which creative format Which image and which copy performed best for that particular audience? And so the way I did that was to set up a range of different formats creatives images, etc And to literally test those within the same ad set And so what we what I kind of did is run that over a period of time as you can see here these three ads Have had spend the other three ads haven't before ads haven't had spend yet They're in the background waiting and I wouldn't run necessarily them all together for this particular one I in general I run three ads at a time. So if there's a bad performer, I'll cut it I'll bring another ad in if there's a good performer Then I graduate it from my testing from my page post engagement into my main account main campaign So that might be my website conversion for example So here for example what I do and this is using the ad sniper strategy Which you'll come across or may have already seen in the training is I look at the average for the ad set and Then I look at the ads within that to find which are Above or below average. So when I'm doing my creative fishing the metrics Primarily looking at our cost per click and click through rate What's the reason for looking at those metrics? Especially within page post engagement actually it comes down to The fact that page post engagement the objective itself is looking for people who are likely to Click into the ad and it's just a general click Maybe share or comment or like for example So it's a real kind of broad objective But what I find and what works for me is if I run an audience and a creative in page post engagement And I get clicks through to my site That is such a strong indication that that ad has hit hit the right kind of tone for that audience as well So even though it's going out quite broad if I get an ad with a low cost per click and a decent click through rate Which is defined by what you know about your audience So, you know, I can't tell you that this is what the cost per click should be This is what the CTR should be because it's all relative to your account. But for example in this one here I know that a 50 pence cost per click or thereabouts is really good and and kind of anything above I think on this one is something like anything above 1.2 1.3 percent of good. So this ad set is doing okay However, what I also do is to look at the metrics further down the funnel Now you may not have a funnel, especially if it's just a landing page or the email capture But if you do then have a look at how far people are getting down your funnel as well So this is an e-commerce site. We've been doing testing over a period of time
